content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 4, 1993

Larry Gross, Executive Director
Coalition for Economic Survival
1296 N. Fairfax Ave.
Los Angeles, CA  90046

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-128

Dear Mr. Gross:

You have requested advice on behalf of the Coalition for
Economic Survival concerning the campaign disclosure provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTION

Did the Coalition for Economic Survival (the "Coalition")
incur campaign disclosure obligations when it produced a brochure
in connection with the Los Angeles mayoral election?

CONCLUSION

The brochure clearly indicates that the Coalition supports
and opposes specific candidates running in the election.
Therefore, if the Coalition received contributions totaling $1,000
or more to produce and distribute the brochure, it will be
required to file campaign disclosure reports.

FACTS

The Coalition is a grass-roots community organization whose
purpose is to organize tenants to secure their rights and
affordable housing. According to the information you provided by
telephone to Lynda Cassady of the Commission staff, the Coalition
solicited and received donations totaling more than $1,000 to
produce a brochure which was mailed to renters in the Los Angeles
area prior to the recent mayoral election. The brochure contains

1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Larry Gross
Our File No. A-93-128
Page 2


an "L.A. Mayoral Renters' Rights Report Card" which summarizes the
voting records and other activities of seven mayoral candidates
with regard to renters' issues and then grades each candidate.
One candidate received an A-, one a B; two candidates received D's
and three received F's.

ANALYSIS

Entities that qualify as "committees" under the Act are
required to file periodic reports disclosing contributions
received and expenditures made for the purpose of influencing the
outcome of elections. "Committee" is defined in Section 82013 to
mean:

...any person or combination of persons who directly
or indirectly does any of the following:

(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year.
(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or
(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or at
the behest of candidates or committees.

The terms "contribution" and "expenditure" are defined in
Sections 82015 and 82025, respectively, as payments which are made
for political purposes. A payment is made for "political
purposes" if it is for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination
or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or
passage of any measure. (Regulation 18215(a)(1).)

In addition, the term "expenditure" includes payments for
communications which expressly advocate the nomination, election
or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or candidates, or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified ballot
measure. A communication "expressly advocates" the nomination,
election or defeat of a candidate if it refers to a clearly
identified candidate or measure so that the communication, taken
as a whole, unambiguously urges a particular result in an
election. (Regulation 18225(b)(2).)

The brochure produced by the Coalition clearly urges voters
to vote for the candidates who received the highest "grades" in
the report card and not to vote for the candidates who received
the lowest. Therefore, the Coalition received "contributions" for
the purpose of making "expenditures" as those terms are defined in
the Act and Commission regulations. Because the Coalition
received contributions totaling $1,000 or more, it qualifies as a
"recipient committee" under Section 82013(a).

Larry Gross
Our File No. A-93-128
Page 3


As a recipient committee, the Coalition must file periodic
reports disclosing contributions received and expenditures made in
connection with production of the brochure. In addition, if the
Coalition produced the brochure independent of the candidate it
supported, the Coalition has made "independent expenditures" and
may be required to file supplemental independent expenditure
reports and late independent expenditure reports.


An "independent expenditure" is a payment made for a
communication which expressly advocates the nomination, election
or defeat of a clearly identified candidate, but which is not made
to or at the behest of the affected candidate. (Section 82031.)
A payment is made "at the behest" of a candidate if it is made
under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with, or at the request or
suggestion of the candidate or the candidate's controlled
committee. (Regulation 12215(b).)

Enclosed is the Commission's information manual, which
describes when reports must be filed and the types of information
required to be disclosed, and the appropriate reporting forms. If
you have additional questions, please do not hesitate to contact
us at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

Enclosures
